REAL PROPERTY/HOUSING MOTION 501, DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT IS SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND AND WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. THE DEMURRER TO THE FIRST CAUSE OF ACTION FOR CREDITOR'S SUIT IS SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND FOR PLAINTIFF TO ALLEGE WHICH DEFENDANTS HAVE POSSESSION OR CONTROL OF WHICH PROPERTIES; WHEN THE PROPERTIES WERE PURCHASED AND WHO PURCHASED THEM; AND WHO THEY WERE TRANSFERRED TO AND WHEN. THE DEMURRER TO THE SECOND CAUSE OF ACTION FOR QUIET TITLE IS MOOT. THE DEMURRER TO THE THIRD CAUSE OF ACTION FOR FRAUDULENT TRANSFER IS OVERRULED. THE DEMURRER TO THE FOURTH CAUSE OF ACTION CONSPIRACY TO DEFRAUD IS SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. THE ALLEGATIONS IN 34 RELATING TO THE CONSPIRACY TO TRANSFER 547 23RD AVENUE REFERENCES THE YANS AND DEFENDANTS FANG AND TU ONLY. PLAINTIFF TO ALLEGE SPECIFIC FACTS TYING DEFENDANT T24 LLC TO A CONSPIRACY WITH RESPECT TO THE PROPERTY. PLAINTIFF MUST ALLEGE WHEN HE DISCOVERED THE FACTS CONSTITUTING THE FRAUDULENT TRANSFER. (CC 338(D).) THE DEMURRER TO THE FIFTH CAUSE OF ACTION FOR THEFT IS SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. PLAINTIFF CANNOT SET FORTH FACTS ESTABLISHING THE FIRST ELEMENT OF THE CLAIM: THAT THE PROPERTY HAS BEEN STOLEN OR HAS BEEN OBTAINED IN ANY MANNER CONSTITUTING THEFT. (PENAL CODE 496, SUBD. (A).) THE ELEMENT REQUIRES THAT WHEN THE PROPERTY IN QUESTION CAME INTO THE DEFENDANT'S HANDS, IT ALREADY HAD THE CHARACTER OF HAVING BEEN STOLEN. PLAINTIFF'S STATUS AS A JUDGMENT CREDITOR DOES NOT TURN HIM INTO THE OWNER OF MR. YAN'S PROPERTIES SUCH THAT HE CAN MAINTAIN A CAUSE OF ACTION FOR THEFT OF HIS PROPERTY. JUDGE: CHARLES F. HAINES; CLERK: L. TAUALA ; NOT REPORTED. (D501)

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Aug-05-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT NOBLE LANE, LLC's MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Specially appearing defendant Noble Lane LLC moves to quash service of the summons and complaint and for dismissal pursuant to CCP 583.250. The motion is denied.

"When a defendant challenges the court's personal jurisdiction on the ground of improper service of process, the burden is on the plaintiff to prove the existence of jurisdiction by proving, inter alia, the facts requisite to an effective service." (Summers v. McClanahan (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 403, 413 [simplified].)

Plaintiff Bryant Fu provides multiple proofs of service for Noble Lane LLC. One is a proof of service on Tony Li at 27479 Ponderosa Ct, Hayward, CA 94545 on April 12, 2025, as the agent for service of process of Noble Lane; the declarant is a registered process server. The other is a proof of service on John Doe, a facility manager, as substitute service on John Wynn. This second proof of service says that Wynn is Noble Lane's agent for service of process and that service was effected at 478 East Santa Clara Street 95112. No declaration of diligence is provided. This POS is dated March 10, 2025. A third proof of service is provided showing service on John Wynn as agent for service of process by substituted service on Jane Doe at 426 S. Main St., Milpitas. No declaration of diligence is provided; this POS is dated March 11, 2025.

Noble Lane provides a declaration from John Wynn who states that he is its former agent for service of process. He states that there is no facility manager at 478 East Santa Clara Street. He states that 426 Main Street in Milpitas is not an address he is affiliated with. He does not state whether he received the summonses.

Noble Lane provides a declaration from Tony Li stating he has no connection with 27479 Ponderosa Court in Hayward. His supplemental declaration supplies proof of another address.

The court takes judicial notice of the Secretary of State's online record that Tony Li is the agent for service and that the service address is 478 East Santa Clara Street, Suite 235, San Jose, CA 95112. The declaration of Bryant Fu establishes that the previous service agent was John Wynn at that address, and that Wynn was the agent for service as of March 10, 2025. John Wynn does not deny that he actually received the summons and complaint at that address, and a dispute or mistake over whether there is a facility manager is not dispositive of whether service was proper. Moreover, the court finds that Fu's proof of service is in substantial compliance with the statutory service requirements, Noble Lane has actual notice of this action, and Fu has provided strong indication that Noble Lane was attempting to evade service, such as by including a fictional suite number in its registered service address.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Declaring Plaintiff Bryant Fu A Vexatious Litigant, For Security, And For Prefiling Order
Off Calendar Aug-05-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT VI DAN TRAN's Motion For An Order Declaring Plaintiff Bryant Fu A Vexatious Litigant, For Security, And For Prefiling Order.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Vi Dan Tran's motion for an order declaring plaintiff Bryant Fu a vexatious litigant and for related relief is denied.

The definition of vexatious litigant in CCP 391(b) includes a person who: "(1) In the immediately preceding seven-year period has commenced, prosecuted, or maintained in propria persona at least five litigations other than in a small claims court that have been (i) finally determined adversely to the person or (ii) unjustifiably permitted to remain pending at least two years without having been brought to trial or hearing." Tran contends that Fu is a vexatious litigant because he has, while representing himself, lost four litigations in the last seven years and in the fifth, the present action, he inexplicably did not serve most of the defendants or move the action forward for a period of at least two years.

The court grants the unopposed requests for judicial notice and takes judicial notice of the entire docket in the cases filed with this court. It appears that in case number CGC-18-572347, Fu sued three defendants and all were dismissed (either through his voluntary dismissal, which counts as an adverse determination for purposes of the vexatious litigant statute, see Karnazes v. The Lauriedale Homeowners Assn. (2023) 96 Cal.App.5th 275) or after a demurrer). Similarly, CGC-20-586049 involves Fu's dismissal of the entire case against multiple defendants without prejudice, before he filed proofs of service of any of the defendants. Fu dismissed his involuntary bankruptcy petition against Tina Yan.

The parties dispute whether the bankruptcy case counts in light of the unique nature of bankruptcy and the circumstances of that dismissal. The court does not need to reach that question because it determines that Fu is not a vexatious litigant within the meaning of the statute regardless if the bankruptcy action counts as an adverse final determination. CCP 391(b) does not refer to five defendants; it refers to five "litigations." The court construes the statute to require separate cases. At most, Tran has shown three cases. Moreover, whether a litigant is vexatious is a matter entrusted to the court's discretion. (Garcia v. Lacey (2014) 231 Cal.App.4th 402, 407.) Garcia's reference to judicial discretion indicates that the court should give some consideration to the circumstances of adverse determinations. Here, the court takes judicial notice that Fu recovered significant costs against William Mora in the CGC-18-572347 matter after prevailing at trial on Mora's cross-claim; that Fu's involuntary bankruptcy petition, although dismissed, came after his recovery of a significant judgment against Yan in an earlier case; and that the CGC-20-586049 action did not require the defendants or the court to expend unnecessary resources dealing with a futile case because Fu dismissed it without serving the defendants or bringing any motions. It does not appear to this court that Fu meets the statutory definition of vexatious litigant.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Aug-05-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT LEGAL RECOVERY, LLC's MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Specially appearing defendant Legal Recovery LLC moves to quash plaintiff Bryant Fu's service of summons and moves for dismissal pursuant to CCP 583.250 (prescribing dismissal if service is not made within three years). The motion is denied.

"When a defendant challenges the court's personal jurisdiction on the ground of improper service of process, the burden is on the plaintiff to prove the existence of jurisdiction by proving, inter alia, the facts requisite to an effective service." (Summers v. McClanahan (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 403, 413 [simplified].)

Here, Fu has filed a proof of service showing service on Legal Recovery LLC's agent for service of process, Demas Yan (aka Dennis Yan). The court takes judicial notice of the Secretary of State's online record of Legal Recovery's agent as Demas Yan. Fu also provides a declaration from himself and his father, Tony Fu, further attesting to service. Yan provides a declaration stating that no service occurred. But the proof of service is proper on its face, complies with statutory requirements for service, and is supported by declarations carrying Fu's burden. Moreover, Legal Recovery has actual notice of this lawsuit. (Cf. Ramos v. Homeward Residential, Inc. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 1434, 1443 ["As stated in the Nov. 25, 1968, Report of the Judicial Council's Special Committee on Jurisdiction, pp. 14-15: 'The provisions of this chapter should be liberally construed to effectuate service and uphold the jurisdiction of the court if actual notice has been received by the defendant, and in the last analysis the question of service should be resolved by considering each situation from a practical standpoint. ...' "].)

Because the court finds the March 10, 2025 proof of service sufficient to afford personal jurisdiction over Legal Recovery, it does not address Legal Recovery's request for dismissal under the three-year service statute.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Off Calendar Nov-18-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT VI DAN TRAN's Motion To Admit Surety.

No appearances are made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant and cross-complainant Vi Dan Tran's motion to admit surety is denied without prejudice.

Plaintiff and cross-defendant Bryant Fu commenced an action on April 27, 2022. Defendant and cross-complainant Vi Dan Tran filed a cross-complaint for conspiracy on fraudulent conveyances and aiding and abetting fraud. Tran alleges that Fu and his mother, cross-defendant Crystal Lei, participated in a conspiracy to fraudulently conceal third party Thomas Fu's assets. Fu and Lei filed a motion for an order declaring Tran a vexatious litigant and requiring the posting of a security pursuant to CCP 391. This Court granted that motion, declaring Tran a vexatious litigant and ordering him to post a security of $150,000 or else his cross-complaint be dismissed. Instead of posting a security directly with the Court, Tran filed the instant motion seeking to admit a personal surety to satisfy the security requirement.

CCP 995.010 to 996.560 codify the requirements for bonds and undertakings. CCP 995.510 delineates the requirements of a personal surety: (1) the surety must be a person other than the principal, and not an officer of the court or a member of the State Bar, (2) the surety must be a resident, and either an owner of real property or householder, within the state, (3) the surety must be worth the amount of the bond in real or personal property, or both, situated in this state, over and above all debts and liabilities, exclusive of property exempt from enforcement of a money judgment. (CCP 995.510(a).) In addition, if the amount of a bond exceeds ten thousand dollars ($10,000) and is executed by more than two personal sureties, the worth of a personal surety may be less than the amount of the bond, so long as the aggregate worth of all sureties executing the bond is twice the amount of the bond. (CCP 995.510(b).)

The Yan and Lau declarations indicate that the individuals reside at the subject property, but do not indicate whether the subject property is their principal residence. (Declaration of Leeds Disston, Exhibit 2.) Without this information, the court is unable to determine whether the personal surety complies with CCP 995.510(a), as the subject property may be exempt from the enforcement of a money judgment. (CCP 704.710 to 704.850.)

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: S. Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)

DEFENDANT VI DAN TRAN MOTION TO EXPUNGE LIS PENDENS (PER 170.6 CHALLENGE TO JUDGE HAINES)
Off Calendar Sep-08-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT VI DAN TRAN MOTION TO EXPUNGE LIS PENDENS (PER 170.6 CHALLENGE TO JUDGE HAINES)

Defendant Tran's motion to expunge lis pendens is DENIED. Motions to expunge lis pendens are governed by Code of Civil Procedure sections 405.30 to 405.39. Section 405.30 allows any party to an action or, alternatively, any nonparty with an interest in the real property affected thereby to seek to expunge a lis pendens. It is true that Defendant Tran did not make a prima facie showing that he has an interest in the subject real property, but indisputably he is a party to this action. Section 405.31 provides, "In proceedings under this chapter, the court shall order a lis pendens expunged if it finds the pleading on which the lis pendens is based does not contain a real property claim." A real property claim is one that, if meritorious, would affect the title to, or the right of possession of, specific real property. (Code Civ. Proc., 405.4.) The relevant pleading here contains a real property claim. Plaintiff Fu alleges that an interest in each of the three properties went from judgment debtors Demas Yan and/or Tina Yan to others by way of a, or a series of, fraudulent transfers and, as relief, Fu seeks to have the transfers voided. (See SAC at pp. 9-11.) The court must order that the notice be expunged if it finds that the claimant has not established by a preponderance of the evidence the probable validity of the real property claim. (Code Civ. Proc., 405.32; Ziello v. Superior Court (1995) 36 Cal.App.4th 321, 331-32.) Here, Plaintiff Fu has established by a preponderance of the evidence the probable validity of his fraudulent transfer claim. (Fu Decl., 4-39 & exs. 1-26.) This is the second time the court has made this determination on a substantially identical record in this case. (See Order Denying Defendant's Motion to Expunge Lis Pendens, filed Aug. 17, 2023.)

Defendant Tran's request that the court strike Plaintiff Fu's opposition is DENIED. The rules regarding e-service upon a party who is e-filing and, themselves, e-serving, are a bit opaque (see Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.251(b)(&(c); LRSF, rule 2.11(D)&(Q)) and, here, Defendant failed to establish prejudice from being e-served.

Proposed order submitted for review.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/JMQ)

Argued. After reviewing the pleadings and listening to oral arguments adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant's Demurrer to the Amended Complaint is SUSTAINED with leave to amend and without leave to amend.

The demurrer to the first cause of action for Creditor's Suit is sustained with leave to amend for Plaintiff to allege which defendants have possession or control of which properties; when the properties were purchased and who purchased them; and who they were transferred to and when.

The demurrer to the second cause of action for Quiet Title is moot.

The demurrer to the third cause of action for Fraudulent Transfer is overruled.

The demurrer to the fourth cause of action Conspiracy to Defraud is sustained with leave to amend. The allegations in 34 relating to the conspiracy to transfer 547 23rd Avenue references the Yans and defendants Fang and Tu only. Plaintiff to allege specific facts tying Defendant T24 LLC to a conspiracy with respect to the property. Plaintiff must allege when he discovered the facts constituting the fraudulent transfer. (CC 338(d).)

The demurrer to the fifth cause of action for Theft is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff cannot set forth facts establishing the first element of the claim: that the property has been stolen or has been obtained in any manner constituting theft. (Penal Code 496, subd. (a).) The element requires that when the property in question came into the defendant's hands, it already had the character of having been stolen. Plaintiff's status as a judgment creditor does not turn him into the owner of Mr. Yan's properties such that he can maintain a cause of action for theft of his property.
